speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This third conversation with Eugene Settle takes place underneath the big willow tree in the backyard at his home in Moscow on August 4th, 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, we used to have lots of orchards out here. You think up here on Orchard Avenue runs out through there now. No, the trail out there is for two miles from about there. And that was nothing but, that was nothing but old orchards. And that was in, now we, our place was just over from Orchard Avenue. We used to own the place. That's where I grew up at you might say. And that was about 1912. And then, I don't remember when they started. It must have been right after World War I I think is when they started. Around the early 20s, then they started to pull them out. We used to have a vinegar plant here on North Main Street here in the early days. And they used to go out there and go flying around. That's when they'd haul their apples into this vinegar plant here. They didn't sell them on the market locally. They'd just haul them in, wagon loads of them, haul them into the vinegar plant here. And last, I think the vinegar plant burned down. I'm not sure about that. Because of the fire, I don't think they never did repair it. It was right in there where the tractor company is at now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know where they sent that vinegar? Was it just the vinegar?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, I don't know where they did. I imagine they shipped it all over, I imagine. I don't know why they marked it from here. But I imagine they shipped it all over.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't know whether it's like the pea now. You see, Moscow is supposed to be one of the dry pea capital of the world. And somebody, I don't know whether, went over and was over in, I don't know whether it was Sweden or Norway.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And they bought some good peas and they had a good pantry here in Moscow.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So they still have a part of the vinegar, it has that wire. Wire, see, nothing. And we used to have a flour mill down here. And of course, I don't know why, that had quite a wide range of flour. I know the fellas talk about buying Bluestem flour. They found that the flour they paid was Bluestem, they called it. And the fellas said that he'd been up in Montana and said that you could buy it up there. It's just as cheap up there as you could right here at the mill. So since I put in about 20 years in the grain business, I found out why they market stuff. Just like wheat. If you go down here to one of these grain elevators and sell a load of wheat, and then you turn right around and buy two bushels of it back, to this cost, what you pay for it, the freight to the coast is added to the price of it. If you buy it back, well they add the freight to it to the coast, it's just up that much. And so that's the way it's at. Well, the same way as when I was down in Virginia here, I guess it was in 62, I went to Lumberyard down there with my brother, he was doing some wheeling.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: This knotty pine lumber was cheaper down there, I could buy it cheaper down there than it could right here in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that shipping trouble was a big problem?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, you know it cost them something to ship it down there, and that has added to it. But I think in that case though, I believe it was just the dealer margin was just higher here, and they just get a bigger rake off here than down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was western white pine?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, this may have come from the lowest of mill. That's why I priced it, I wanted to see the price. I was just trying to see if I had my price on the board down. Western white pine, I asked them where it was from, and they said it was shipped from the West, in Boston. And I asked them what the price was, and then I came back home and I priced it here, and it was cheaper, really cheaper, and it was cheaper there than it was here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I think it was a rake off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember where the markets were when your father was farming? Where they were shipping their grain to?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I don't know where they were. But I imagine it's pretty much like it is now, because I know that when I was a little kid, they used to claim that Russia controlled the world wheat market. It seemed like when Russia had a big crop, our prices were down in states, because we were competing with Russia, you see. I guess it was low cost of labor and one thing or another, like it is now. But I heard the farmer say that if they had a big crop in Russia, it was a lookout for us, we'd have a big wheat plant.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By the way, have you heard anything from your daughter about that old records that you made out there?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I haven't.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I thought maybe I wrote to her about it when she came. We bought a car for our granddaughter, and they supposed to come after it around the first of this month, and I told them that if she found that, to bring it with her, but she hasn't showed up yet. I don't know what you think about that. Ask her to do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you fill me in more on your grandfather, from what you remember?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because we didn't talk about him hardly because of that, and I'm not sure that if you don't get that soon. I'd really like to know some about, did you hear about what his...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I know you didn't know him. No, I never knew my grandfather on my dad's side at all. I don't remember my grandmother either, but she was still alive when I was a baby, but I don't remember her. I took my sister down there when we were in Portland here about a month ago, and she said she remembers her well. She remembers her grandmother real well. But I don't know whether she remembers my grandfather on the father's side or not. Of course, we left my grandfather's place to come out here. When we left that country, we came by and stopped at my grandfather's, my mother's folks, and we went to the arrival of one of my brothers before we came on west. But my father's people, I don't remember. I remember one of his sisters. We met his sisters after one of his, and I said we was on our way out here, one of his sisters. Then I met one of his cousins, first cousins, two of his cousins out down in Los Angeles since I've been a man. I was down there in 54, it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know about how old he would have been when he got freed from slavery? How old?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Grandfather.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old he would have been?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, I don't have no idea how old he would have been when he was freed. I don't have no idea.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: First he was just, I'm sure he was just a young man.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I presume in his early 20s, I imagine, I don't know, but I don't have no idea.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he stayed?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't know. Well, that's just my focus, but I don't think my daddy, I never did hear him say. That's something I never heard him say how old his father was when he was born.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he stayed in the same area or did he go someplace else?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, see that after the Civil War ended, he was in North Carolina.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: After the war ended, he got a job in a little one -horse little sawmill in the hills of North Carolina. I don't remember what the place was, but that was his first job after he was a free man. He went to work in this sawmill in North Carolina. And my grandmother, she never was a slave because she was Indian. But she was kind of a little maid there for this sawmill owner. She helped around the house and took care of the kids. She was kind of a mistress to look after the kids around the place there. I don't know how long she had been with them. But anyway, that's her job. She lived there with them and took care of the kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that's where they met?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, uh -huh.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then they married and then went... And it seemed like then they left there right away and went to Mississippi. I don't know what the attraction was to Mississippi, taking from North Carolina, but anyway they went to Mississippi.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, my mother lived right not too far from Corinth, Mississippi, and they wasn't too far apart. And there they raised the Mississippi's. I think there's about three boys and about four or five girls in my father's family.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he had one brother that was older than he, and I don't know how many sisters.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Anyway, that's when they were living in Mississippi. The family grew up there and raised their family in Mississippi. And then after... In fact, after they had raised a big family there...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's when my grandmother, they got... They were pretty old people, old people. I guess they must have been old people.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Then... Well, she put in for her a lot, but in the Indian territory. And then they went out, and then that's just about the time my dad and mother married. A little before that was...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Before that was... Because when...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: When my grandfather passed away out there... We went out there, my dad and mother, they went out there to live with my grandmother, take the other place.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And see, my sister was... I think she was pretty near two years old when I went out there because I was... More than that, they went out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were born in Oklahoma, too?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I was born in the Indian territory. You think your father...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, then he went out maybe because your grandfather had died to take care of your grandmother?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, that's why he went out there. He went out to own this place there. And because my grandmother, my sister was talking about it from the way I found there. She lived there for a year.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, he worked there for... The place there wasn't much to it to see. All the good land was taken by that time, you see. Of course, see, that's still open. I believe it was about 1868, I think is when it was still open, see. So about all the good land was taken. Of course, there's oil on the place that we had. What she had there, but I don't know. That didn't even discover until about 19.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Must have been around about 19, 17, 18, maybe, or maybe even that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Anyway, my dad stayed there for about a year or better. My grandmother died shortly after we went there, and then he left the place. He left it before my grandmother died. This rock pile, he called it, you could make a living on that. So he had a good team and wagon, and he went to Arkansas, Fort Smith, Arkansas. Then my grandmother passed over here, and then we went out there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Then from there, that's when I told you about, he heard about the great Northwest, and that's when he brought him west from there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were telling me before, you mentioned this, that he did a lot of hunting in those days. Yes, he did. He was quite a hunter.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He had lots of hound dogs, and he hunted. I remember when he was in Arkansas, he lived on the farm along Arkansas River. He had, I don't know how many dogs he had, he had quite a bunch of hounds. He hunted a lot, and then people would come out from Fort Smith, businessmen and wives would come out and try to pay him so much that him and his hound dogs go coon hunting possibly along the Mississippi River. He brought up, yeah he brought every weekend, every so often. He always took a bunch of them and came out and went hunting every night, you know.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He was quite a hunter, and that's one thing that is kind of exciting about coming west, he thought that he'd lost a game out here, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go hunting with him very much when you were growing up here?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yeah, we hunted deer lots. He had, he rode hounds out here with him. We used to hunt coyotes, yeah. We used to hunt coyotes all over. We'd spare at ICO and all, a bunch of these volunteers. We'd hunt coyotes and hunting. Yeah, we hunted. We did lots of hunting after he came west, but that is for coyotes. I don't believe he ever, I don't believe he ever did go deer or elk hunting. He never did, I don't think. We hunted lots. We used to hunt coyotes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That sounds like that would be pretty hard kind of hunting to me. I mean, those coyotes are pretty wily animals.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, well, yeah they are, but they used to get after them with dogs, you know. And the idea was, it was just kinda, after the dogs get after them, one of them would get out on some point or another, and probably wait for them to bring them around and gunshot. Sometimes they'd take them, grab them, come to some kind of run in a circle in different ways, you know. I don't know, my dad, he hunted, but he hunted, and he always, with his dogs, he never had nothing but a shotgun, and he was number six shot. So, you know, you gotta get pretty close to, I don't know, kill us, number six shot. But he just gotta learn how to, I don't know. And so, I was a Moscow guy, I used to go out and get my dog, and my dad would go out hunting, and they used to come out on Saturdays and days in wintertime, and Chase Coyote was just quite a sport then. Then different ones around here got different than dogs, or I don't know whether you heard of the Weeks, this or not, they were, they were southerners, and they had hounds, and they did a lot of hunting. I guess he was the last one. In the Weeks, I don't know whether he's the last of that generation, he had hounds, but he still got anything out. I haven't seen him for a long time. He always had dogs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you would go hunting, did you go for a day, or would you even camp out?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, just go for a day. Oh, yeah, I used to, of course, I was about the only member of our family that ever went deer and elk hunting. I used to do that, go up and sail away on the Lochsa River, and we'd go in, go fair, and probably, in a couple of years, probably camp out for a week at a time, and it was all elk and deer.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So that's hunting, elk, deer, deer that I hunted. Well, I've hunted them around here quite a lot, which is big joule out here up there south of joules, a little south and west of joule up in there, back up in there. That's pretty good. These white -tailed deer, you know, they're kind of, oh, I don't think they're not domesticated here, but you might find a white -tailed deer out here. Some farmer, Phillip, along with some creek wild wild wild eared brush, you know, they're kind of open -country animals. That's them in the face. Yeah, they've come right downtown Moscow here. You can see the prints now on that store right across, down Washington Street, on the building right off the corner of Washington, and third and Washington right across the post office there. There used to be, this is the store now, I'm pretty sure, old birds used to own it there. They went down there and got on Washington Street there, and they tried to hem it up there, and they tried to jump, climb up the side of that building, and its hoof marks was in that corner of that building right now while that deer was trying to get away and jump up on it. There's a couple in it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, there have been several deer right here in Moscow. I was wondering, wondered how long this year, I think about two years ago there was a deer right out here south of town out there, where that implement snowmobile dealer is out there, and they ran out south of town there. Then they came out and went out there towards the cemetery, just between town and the cemetery there, so they kind of shoot right out here by the fairgrounds.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I guess that was the winter we had, it was in 17, I believe, 72, 71, or something, I believe it was in 71, I think the first one I had left was 71.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do much with your brothers when you were growing up?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yeah, we did lots of hunting, chased around. We'd take the time. On weekends, school was all in school for us on Saturdays, like we'd all get our guns and go to the mountains up here, over there, and chase all the time. We fought outdoors in all those days. We liked the outdoors, liked the hills, chased around lots of times, we did lots of hunting.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: We didn't hang around town too much until we were well grown. We just liked the outdoors and we all had horses and was coming around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted to ask you about the sports that you did in school, too.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, there was only two of us boys, myself and my second brother. We were the only ones that took part in any sports in school. When I was in high school, I was on the track team, and I played some football because I had also a small, I was a lot of hard -to -force stringers. I didn't play very much, but I played some football.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then the brother next to me, my next second brother, he was on the Moscow High School track team. And then I had a nephew that was on the Moscow, was on the high school football team, won the state championship. And then he played freshman football up here at the university, and he got hurt and he didn't go anywhere. But my younger brother, the brother next to me, they never took no part in any sports in high school or otherwise.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was track your favorite?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, that's mine, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like, sports in those days, when you were going to school? I mean, like for track, did you guys have uniforms and go around and play at the schools?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, when I was in high school, Moscow High School just began to come to its own in track because up until 1912, until they built the new, the Whitworth now, right across the list, right up on the same side of the Methodist Church there, that was built in about 1912, and that's when I started there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And up until that time, there was one building there that had their junior high, and high school, and I don't know how many other smaller groups, was in the same building, see?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Because, in fact, all the kids in those days, up until 1912, they had a prep department at the university here, and they all went up there, you see. So high school wasn't big enough until they built the new high school, and when they built the new high school, then they cut out the prep department at the university there, and so the kids, they had to go to high school here, but before that, when they had the prep department at the university there, when you finished eighth grade, well, you go there, and you take your four years high school, and then go right on through college, right down the hill, but when they built this new high school, well, then they cut the prep department out, that's when I came in. And so we were just building, started an athletic program when I started, just began to take hold, because I know in football, they didn't have a department, they couldn't, they didn't finish, we had to furnish our own uniforms. Hmm. Hmm.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then, of course, after that, the athletics began to grow then, of course, they got the athletic department, of course, which is a big deal now. You have everything, you have football, and track, and wrestling, and, I don't know why they have tennis, lots of sports, not a whole category of sports.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you compete in?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I was a distance runner.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I held the record, high school record for the mile for, oh, I don't know how many years, anyway, my brother, he broke it while I was in service.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At least it stayed in family. At least it stayed in family. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, of course, it wasn't, in those days, you know, in the fives,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You did pretty good if you could run a mile in five minutes, that was pretty good. I think this record I set here was five, high school was five -five, but I ran it a little better than that, it was one time, but I wasn't in competition, I was running with the, the kids running the 880 or the half mile, and I run, they run me in the 880 with them, and then they run with me in the mile. Yeah, that's what I put, I had my best record, but of course, that wasn't competition then. It was low or it was five -three something, but the record was, I set when I clocked me, it was five -five. Of course, you run the mile now down, you run it in three something now. So, you see, it wasn't very fast. It was lots of fun though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do a lot of...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: In the wintertime, we'll start early in the spring, they, you see, we didn't have no, they didn't have no regular coach, they didn't hire high school football coach, or track coach. They got some university, junior or senior university that worked us out, see that. You know, they didn't even have a, as I said, the athletic program wasn't much, but they didn't even have their own coach, they just got some kids from the university, junior or senior university to handle them. And they fixed it so in the early spring, why we could have a, we'd go up to university in certain, certain evenings and use the girls' gym up there for our training indoors in the wintertime. And we did our other training. We did, we did up on the university field. Of course we played, we played our football down here at Ghormley Park, down on West 60th, West 60th there. That's where we played the football down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the sports was as important to the schools then as it is now?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well... Of course they built up from there and it is very important at the present time, but it was just, it didn't amount to much when I started high school. Of course I said all the kids went to the university up there, they didn't want it there to take, take a lot of part of them until they felt high school. It was just built up, but from 1912 it started building up and of course by the time my nephew got in high school, well gee, it was a big deal then. And then we played, we just played, well we played schools like Troy and Colfax and all these little ones, Georgetown, Genesee, that. Of course now, in Moscow, they play in a different league altogether like in good, they don't play nobody. Smaller than Lewiston, they play Lewiston and Spokane High Schools and Court Lane and things like that. But when I went to high school there, well we played Oakdale, all these little towns around, Colfax and Troy and wherever they had high school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Seems like nowadays they're big community events too.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes, you bet they are. You bet they're big community events.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder if they were, if it was much that way, did you have a lot of fans coming along to root in those days?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, in those days it just wasn't nothing more than the student body was more, just blood on the bottom of our events. Well unless we had a kind of an interstate track, we had a district track like we had at Lewiston. First time I was ever in Lewiston I was on that track in 2019. That was in 19, spring of 1913 it was, a Lewiston track. And then they had schools there from Boise and that was a big event, it was a big crowd there then.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I ran with a kid down there on Lewiston Normal Hill, Lewiston Normal Hill, it's a state college now. He was from Grangeville in 1913 and in 1919, in May 3, 1919 I ran with him in Paris.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: We ran a relay race from Chateau Thierry to Paris, that's 15 miles.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And we had about 25 guys on each team and I ran, on the team I was on, I ran the last two miles, my last two miles right in the heart of Paris. We finished at the Arc de Triomphe in Paris as our finishing point and I ran the last two miles right down the streets in Paris, Main Street from Paris.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say that's the first time you'd ever been to Lewiston?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, 1913. I guess it didn't travel around. I mean, kids didn't travel around very much in those days.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, well, you know those days there wasn't any cars, those days you remember. You either went horseback or you went in a buggy, and it's over a team. I think it was about my first, my second automobile ride, was about my second one, was I came from Lewiston, from that track meet, I rode back to Moscow with a kid. His dad worked for an implement company down here in Moscow, set up machinery, and he had an old Model T Ford. They furnished him an old Model T Ford that he went around and set up the fire machinery. He came down to the track meet in that 404 roadster and his son and I rode back with him. That was about my second time here. I got my second ride in an automobile.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: My folks got there, first automobile, in the fall of 1919 after I got back from World War I. That's when I drove over on the driver's car in 1919. They bought a car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember that second ride you took? Was that up to Lewiston Hill?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, up to Lewiston Hill.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I imagine that must have been thrilling back in the first cars. Well, you know going down the hill where that spring is down along the, well, there used to be a little store that would sit there. You know, in the early days, in the first Model T Fords, they used to have to get up to that high and then they'd have to change the carburetor on them. Fill them up with water again and change the carburetor, get it down and open it up. I forget what the past is. The old old Model T Fords, the printer had to stop there and change the carburetor on them. You had to just reset them. Yeah, reset them. I don't know what they did. I don't remember what it was when they opened them up or closed them. High altitude, say. High altitude, see, yeah. That's what it was. That's the old Model T. I remember they used to get up that high and the old thing would just be a sub of them steaming. Of course, that was after that quite a while. It used to be boiling, coming up that hill. There was no five percent grade there like it is now. Some of it was pretty steep. It just every so often, in the long run, that you could place where you could pass anybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think people thought of cars when they first came in?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people think they were here to stay? They were scared, didn't they think?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, yeah.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They used to, I know one thing about it, when they first came in, they thought, well shoot, they just figured it. There were just certain times you could drive them, because you see there was no rock roads or no paved roads or nothing. And then it was all dirt roads.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then summertime the dust would get that deep and when it rained, pretty slick.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They couldn't drive. They thought they just figured it out. When the cars come in, then the road improvement program started, see. That's what brought our good roads, is the cars. We got to rocking them and sanding them and then we got to paving them. And the other thing was that what brought good roads, and then when Roosevelt got in, he started, he really hit it on this road for them. He started this, what they call farm to market roads program. Well, the first time I rode in a car in Spokane, there was no pavement from here to Spokane. It was a dirt road all the way. This is my little forest.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I'll never forget it. This kid lived, a bunch of us young men, we went up to Spokane to file on some land that's sown over and over and around the Moses Lake area, over in that dry country. I was drawing, we went up to file to draw, register for the drawing. And I didn't go up on the train and I met these fellas up there when I was coming. They lived over here in Blaine and one of them's dad's pretty well fixed needs. He drove, he brought four of them up. They drove in his dad's old Model A Ford. Model T, I guess it was, Model T Ford. And so I met a Spokane and they said, well, they got me to ride back with them. So I came back, I came back, I came a little back with them. Five of us came back in that one Model T Ford.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I think it took us, I think it took us pretty half a day to come, to help a Spokane down here and that thing. I don't think we, I don't believe we, I don't believe we traveled about 20, 20, 20 miles an hour, maybe 25. But I know we was on the road a long time.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But that is my first, my first long ride in a car, from Spokane to Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get seasick?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I imagine it. That first time in a car, I can't imagine what I would think, I mean, if I was already grown up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's a brand new experience for those days.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, my first ride in a car, we lived way out here in the country and we went to country school over there. And Dips area broke out in the school. And so they closed the school down, because we had the only kids vaccinated. That's when I got my first ride, the doctor come out, the fellow that run the Gritman Hospital down there then. He came out and vaccinated us kids and the neighbors kids down there. We kind of lived off the main drag and that's the reaction is, he give us a ride up the road about a while in his car. That was my first time, I don't know what that was about, the first car I ever saw. Maybe, because I lived out there in the boondocks, I never come to town. I don't even wear a cell phone, just for surfing or something. And he, he load us all up, took us up the road, took us a couple of miles in his, I don't know what he was going to know then. He had the brakes and everything out on the running board. And I don't know, maybe the shift was out, no, I guess the shift wasn't out, the shift was still down in front I guess. I know there's emergency brake and things, those are all out on the running board. The running board had a big toolbox on it. I don't know what that is. Yeah, that is my first ride now, that's the first time I've been in a car, first time I've, that might have been the first time I've ever seen one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a bad epidemic? That dip theory? Was that a bad one?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, it was pretty bad, yeah.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Some little kid come up with the dip theory and so they close the school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it in your school? Was it in your country school?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes, it was in my school out there. They closed the school until all the kids were going up, was vaccinated. And the doctor come out there and I don't know when he got out of the fireplace there, he vaccinated three families up to where we were, close to where we were. And I don't know, we didn't vaccinate the rest of them, but they took them to town, what, but anyway they heated them up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the boy die?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't remember, I don't think so, I don't think he died. He's pretty sick, but I don't think he died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they shut the school down for a long time?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, not very long, I don't remember how long it was.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Just until they, it seems to me like they, it seems to me like the kids got vaccinated, it doesn't seem like they disinfected the school, fumigated the school, I believe, I believe they did, yeah. Then they fumigated the school, they disinfected it, then they let us come back, we were probably awake maybe, I don't remember.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: When you, those were the good old days. Deep theory in the school. Yeah, deep theory in the school, that's what, I believe that's the only time that I remember that anything broke out in the school while they told us, in the country school. Yeah, of course it was.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: We're in the epidemic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was that bad flu epidemic at the beginning of, or during World War I. During World War I, yeah, the flu.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah. Yeah, that was about 1918, I guess, I was in France. My folks all had it. Oh, except my mother. My brother next to me, he is the one who brought it home, he came home with it, he just about died, clearly. Well, sure it was going to lose him, and they all got down, except my mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they still be able to take care of themselves during World War I?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, my mother, and then we had a neighbor down this way from us, he was a Methodist, he used to be, he was the next Methodist minister, and they run the little one horse laundry. Down about a mile this way from us, between us and town. And he came up there and got the laundry, took it down there and did the laundry for mother. And then there was a fellow that worked with my dad, worked for my dad, on his hay baler, he was a transit. And he's coming through the country, and of course he figured, well I go out to Settle's and I'll get a meal or two. Before he moved on, he went out there. And my mother wouldn't let him in the house, she told him he was all down with the flu.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he asked my mother, he says, who's taking care of this talk?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: We only had four or five horses and two or four cows out there on the place. And she said the neighbors come up and take care of them, and bring them up to the door. Well he says, you tell Mr. Settle, if he'll give me enough money so I can buy me a quart of moonshine or whiskey, he says I'll come out here and take care of the chores for you. Well he says you can't stay in the house, you can't stay in the house. Well he says, just try to get some old blankets and something so I can sleep up there in the hay. And so she went in to talk to dad, and that's what he does. So dad gave him some money, and he went downtown and saw where he got it. But he got him some whiskey, and he stayed out there, and stayed up there in the barn.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And mother would cook his breakfast and meal for him and give them to him. He'd take them from the door, he'd go out and eat them outside. You know, he wouldn't allow them in the house. And we got to eating here and back there, and he stayed there until I think my dad got on his feet. One of the boys, anyway, until he got to take care of himself.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: How badly were... And he left, he left, and we never did see him again. And folks often wondered, I wonder if he may have went off to town and left his going, he was heading down towards the lowest and worst warmer. And they'd tell us one of them. He could have went down there in the jungle, the lowest jungle, and got the flu, and maybe died. They never did see him anymore. They were here for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They must have really appreciated that he did that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, they worried about this guy quite a little bit, talked about it many times afterwards. They always kind of liked to have found her, found us on the one. They worried, because he was just a tramp, a transit. He'd get up to Spokane, he was headed down to this lower country, they called it down there where it was warmer. They wondered if he didn't go to the field. Probably went on down there, and maybe after he left there, he may have got the flu, he may have died, but we never did hear.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he worked for your father many times before that?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, he just worked for them that fall, that summer before that on his hay bale. He worked for them that summer while he was bailing hay, and then they always had bails straw late in the fall. But he worked just through the hay, and he didn't... Oh, and then he left there on hay seeds, so he left that and went over in the Yakima country to take in the apple harvest.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then that winter when he was eating his black peas, going hidden down for us and going for the valley down there where it was warmer. I think it was in November. That's when he stopped up there before he came off the folks and get a meal before he went on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long were they down with that?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't remember, they must have been down for...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Probably a couple of weeks or maybe longer, some of them were longer than that. But I don't...seems like my dad, the ones that had it last,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Some had worse than others. But my brother next to me, he had it the worst of any of them. They felt sure that he didn't think he was going to make it. But my dad and my other brothers, I don't think they had it too heavy. They didn't have it too heavy. But I imagine a week or ten days when he was back where he'd get out and help himself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your mother able to nurse the family during this time?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, she took care of all of them. As I say, this family that run the hand laundry down there, they always come up every day and got sheets and get the laundry up for her. She didn't have nothing like that to do, that's all she had to do. Then until this guy came along, the neighbor at Lewis, he'd come up in the morning and feed the stock. She'd take the milk and look it out on the porch and she'd go and milk the cows and bring it back and sit it on the porch. I don't know how long this guy came along, then this guy came along and he took care of it. That swept through the whole country. Yes, it sure did. There's sure lots of people who died here in Moscow. That epidemic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear about all the military boys stationed here that got the flu during that time?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I heard they got it really bad in the units at the university. Yeah, I know there's several of them. They had a unit here at the university. I know there's a couple of kids. I think we used to live with kids together, farmed off together. One of the boys died up here during that time. He is at the university up here. A kid by the name of Clyde Madison. He lived throughout the country. I think there's several of them that died up here at the university. But I don't know any of the other camps. I don't remember. I don't remember. Like Camp Lewis in North Face. I don't know whether it gets bad out in North Face or not. I don't remember. I don't know whether. I know that we didn't have it in France. I had it after. I think it was the first or second winter I was home after I got home. I had it. It got very bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many guys passing through the country who were tramps like him?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yes, yes there was. Lots of them.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Lots of them. Lots of them.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, you go out here to the country or if you lived along by the railroad track you'd see the fellas with packs on their backs. Lots of tramps. You see a freight train going through one of those usually a few or three tramps on top. It's a variety. Lots of tramps.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah. You take, you know, you used to be a farmer coming to town looking for harvest time. Looking for a man to work. You go down along the jungle down along the railroad tracks. Last time you'd find someone to pick up a good man. They'd just kind of follow the harvester around. You know, they'd camp out down along the railroad tracks. That's the best thing.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Last time you know there's these dozen men down there. Camping along the back of the railroad tracks down there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: For instance.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But now.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: There's no trains, no freight trains from the ride. Yeah, of course you watch for them. You see them packing. Of course they're hippies.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't know what's, I guess his time got better but I don't know if I can see it even way out the country that way. They're so awful. There's always some bum come to the door looking for handout or some kind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do people usually give it to them or turn them away?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: My mother's pretty good about that. She had some. She'd always give them a sandwich or something. Someone would often cut wood or spit wood or something away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted to ask you, Gene, a few more things about racial prejudice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because, well, you know I've heard about it and that it existed around here. I know it did. And I figured that what you would have seen or experienced here would be a good measure of weather, of how much there was. But you said to me last time I was here that it was worse in the old days. Yeah, I think it's worse in the old days than it is now.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But of course in my case,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I grew up on a farm you might say. A kid laid out on a farm and went to a country school.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And of course nationally it wasn't as, I didn't like being in town. I was so many different kids. But of course when I came to high school here though, I was only black in the school system.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you say?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I didn't hear the last thing.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: When I started school here in Moscow, I was the only black kid in the school system at that time. And of course when I came in school all eyes were on me. And it wasn't long before some of the kids were gonna speak to me.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: While others were just gonna shy around me like I was.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But, it was a very short time.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I guess they found out that I was just like anybody else, only my skin was dark. Well they and I, they started falling, kids falling in with me and I just said I was very popular in high school. Because the kids,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They invited me, I guess to come out and turn out for football. I didn't know nothing about football. They kept defending it and I thought it turned out I was football in the spring and I started off the track. I never did play, I played basketball though, that is in a class, college school. I never played basketball, it was too small.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But now whether or not, if I went to different restaurants and been turned down or not, or hotels, I can't say. Because I never had that experience.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have, when you left the area, let's say you went other places or left Moscow and the area, was it any different?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I went, well. I don't mean in France, but I mean just around the north. I know what you mean.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, to tell the truth about it, I never went anywhere. Like when I was in high school, I went with the track team. Yeah, high school. And I guess Lewiston was about the only place I went then where we stayed all night and stayed out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that you would have to prove yourself more than a white would because you were different?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's true to this day, no.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: In this respect, now say for instance that... Well, you just say for instance on the job market.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well... Now you say you and I went and applied for a job. Well,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: The only way that I would have a chance against you getting this job is that I had a far superior record than you had. I might get into that, but just take this. If on the other basis, if I was inexperienced and you was inexperienced, well of course I wouldn't get, you would get the job. But if I had a record superior to yours, well they'd probably give me the job, I would probably get the job over you. But if we went in on the same level, well you know, of course I'd be the guy they turned down. Well, sir, you take the black man today, he's got to have a, usually a superior record, an outstanding record before he has any chance at all to get the white man on the same level.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember ever running into that around here?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, but now I went, now after I finished high school, I took my first, I took a business course the first year I was a bookkeeper. And of course, I tried several places here in Moscow, that's different places where chances to get a job, but I never had no luck getting any work.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Of course I don't remember many of the kids, only in my class they did get in, but I just cried out a time or two for work, but of course if the crowd wasn't as much as they are now, I never got any work.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Of course they didn't tell me it was only counting my color, but they just told me there was nothing, they didn't have anything on it. Well, some people thought that I was well known around Moscow, I might have a pretty good chance of getting a job, but I didn't get nothing. But as I say, I don't remember any of the kids in my class who graduated, but did go to work around Moscow the time they did, but I think there's two of the girls who went to work for a telephone company, just as that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was this?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, that was just before World War I.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: After I got to come back from the Army, there was no GI Bill like they got now for returning veterans. And so after year two, I decided, well actually I had a, there was a doctor in Spokane, he was a meeting inspector.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And besides that, he was a, he took civil service exams, and then he started to be a railway mail clerk.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he came into Moscow here at the time too, and I never met him down here, I met him in Spokane after that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I had a friend, a friend of mine is working in the post office, and he's telling me about this fellow, this fellow, this black man was a railway mail clerk running out of Spokane down to Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so I just thought, well, why the hell is there a chance for me?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I'd like to get me a job, to go into civil service, which is not a different, not just, no discrimination. So then I, that's, I studied for, I got me some,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Went to the library and got civil service books, started brushing up once, you know.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I took the examination, of course at that time I was of course my wife here.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And after I took the examination, I told her about it, what I did, and I thought she'd be very happy about it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But, she's on a contract, she didn't like the idea, because she said, if you go on the railroad, you'll be gone half, two thirds of the time, you won't be home, you'll be gone. And she said, I would much rather, that if we went to farming, we went to farming, and she said we could make it together. So I went, I rented a farm that fall.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then the next summer, the next one, we was married, and I just as walked, a month or six weeks after we was married, I got my call. And I turned it down.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And the kid that took the examination with me the same time, he got his call, and he went,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I, just like I was supposed to do, I was supposed to report to Spokane, Turbo.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he went to, he went to, he went to Spokane, and they kept him around the terminal there just for a short while, you know. And then they put him on a run, and he made two shifts up to, we was cut back in Montana, and back. And then he went to work in the terminal, they dropped him, put him in the terminal, and he put in his whole time right there in Spokane at the terminal. He was 20 and retired there. Just made two trips on the railroad, and then he worked in the terminal. I don't suppose I'd have been that lucky, but he was. He made two trips, just put the cut back in Montana, went up one day and came back the next. And then he went to work, put him in the terminal. That was where you sorted the mail on the train when it was... Yeah, yeah, that's when the trains would carry so much mail. That's the mail we had. The favorite train had a mail clerk on it. The passenger train had a mail clerk on it. And of course the mail came into the terminal, and that's how he said it. Oh, I might have went somewhere there. He told me, he said some of the guys come in there that never was on the... So I never followed through with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think then that because you were living in the country and farming most of the time, that that meant that you were protected from having to put up with anything?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I think I missed a lot of prejudice and discrimination.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I think I did. I think I did.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, in fact, I know I did because I'll take the night. My daughter, she went to school in the country, she went to school in another country. Then she went to school in Genesee. And then she finished up here at Moscow High and the university and all that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And she went to California and hired for a job as a teacher. She got a job as a teacher in the Berkeley School.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And she was in her 20s. And her herself, she didn't experience so much discrimination. But she could see it like she had never saw it before, you know. She could see it in action in the city for many places. And she got her first eyes open. That's about so much discrimination. She left home and went to the city. That's when she found out. Well, of course, the way she was situated, she didn't notice it so much. But she could see it in action from where she was at. They kind of opened their eyes to the fact that before she got there, she didn't... discrimination wasn't noticeable to her. Since she got out of Moscow, she got away from home and she could see it. A lot of the times, the most she knew about it was what she'd read and hear. But she really saw it in action and she got away from home in a big city.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But, for instance, if you're thinking about your career and what you wanted to do, well, you had to take that into consideration. That you'd have to, you know, you'd want to find something where you wouldn't be...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You'd have a better chance of getting ahead of it. Well, that's the way we're about the civil service business. Well,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I suppose that if I had a...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's supposed to be graded on your exam.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: The antivirus... I don't think you... I don't think, now I don't remember. But I know I had a wife's cousin. She got a... he got a good job on the highway patrol in California. And he told me when he got in... when he went in... when he went in, took the examinations, he said you didn't have to... you didn't have to put on... you didn't have to put on race, you see. So they didn't know what you was. And I just... I just... I don't think... I don't remember what I did on my examinations up there, no. I had to put on race or something. I don't know. I don't remember that. Of course, I didn't take it but once, but the public... you tell me, you say, well... I says...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you don't get a high enough grade to get a call the first time, why don't you just try it again?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They say you'll make it after a while. Just get your grade.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you don't get a good grade this time, why don't you just try it again?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And you get your grade up there and you get your grade up to the top, go and try it up there, you'll get a call.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, like among the young people that you knew when you were growing up out in the country, I imagine that they, you know, like you say, when you went to school, they all knew that you were black. Sure. But when you were...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: did they ribby about it or joke about it?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Very seldom. The color line, the color was very seldom brought up when I was a kid tonight. So it was very, very seldom.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: It seemed like after... Well, it seemed like the first joke is the worst one. But it seemed like when they find out, just to say, well, my guy, he's just like anybody else, only his skin's black. It seemed like when they found that out, well, then that kind of took care of him. Of course, there's some that actually never... they wouldn't like me on that account because my face was like theirs. While others actually did a thing. But that they're so far outnumbered, in my picture, it wasn't those.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, that's right. That's right, that's the storm. Down here, I got a job in the warehouse.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I worked down here and supported down at the fence plant for a few years. Then I came back home and I got a job working in the elevator.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I worked out that jewel, that elevator, that's where I started in. I worked out one of the seasons. Then that season, the fellow that was a foreman of that plant out there, he quit. He had a farm down there. He quit.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And the manager of the Bay Tall County Grain Wars, he gave me the job as foreman. Well, that caused a little bit of a hard feelings because these fellows had been there longer than I had to see. He passed over them and gave it to me. So that's discrimination in reverse. That caused a little hard feelings, but it didn't last long. Then I worked down there as foreman of that place of business. Then the fellow that was in the bookkeeper for the outfit, the assistant manager, the manager he quit, and the bookkeeper's assistant manager, he moved up the top. When the board of directors meets the electrical manager, they offered him the job. He said, I'll take the job under one consideration. They want to know what it was. He said, if you let me have a warehouse foreman, superintendent, somebody to look after the warehouse. Well, he said, they didn't see nothing wrong with that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And they wanted to know who did they want. He told them me. That made some of them's eyes pop up, but they told them, go ahead, I got the job. Then I worked at the job for, I guess I was there about 16 years as warehouse superintendent.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Then I had as high as 25 or 30 men working for me at one time through the harvest. Let's say I had a warehouse in Moscow, Joel, Troy, Gary, Potlach, Lola, and Estes.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And so they got me a, furnished me a pick up, and that's all I did. I had about as high as 25 or 30 men on payroll.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were the supervisor?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I was the supervisor overall. I did all the firing and hiring. Look, I had a good boss. He had confidence in me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of that work, of being a supervisor?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I didn't have no, I liked it. I was in the sergeant's army. I used to handle men pretty well. I think I was a pretty good guy handling men. And to tell the truth about it, I never had, oh, this, you might have been fellows that didn't like me, didn't care to have a black supervisor on the ground. I was a father, a great, and if I,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I never had no trouble keeping men. Well, so one thing about me, when a man come to ask me for a job, I never cut him off at the box. I says, give me your, give me your telephone number where I can locate you. I say, I'm full handed today, but I says tomorrow I might be short a man. So you tell me where I can locate you. I never did just tell a man no, I didn't have one. I just, guess I would let him, I'd ask him for his telephone number where I could reach him if I needed anyone. I never had no trouble keeping men. I wish I had the crew, I always had one or two men standing by where I could go get them if I needed them. I had very little trouble. Of course not. I figure that is on account of my boss.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: If they went, anybody went to him about anything, he says, you go to Gene, he's your boss. You go to him.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And, he never went over my decisions.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: If I brought the man, I sent a man to the office his time, I get no questions asked, he just got his check and that was it. He never went over my head. I made the decision in my part of the business, well that is it. That's the one that I'm in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would be the kind of difficulty that would make you fire a man? What would be the kind of reason that you would find you would have to fire a man?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, it's rare for you to suppose I am armed, but there's some, you get cherishedness. But, I don't believe there's, I don't think that, I don't believe there's one or two men, 15, 16 years or 20 years, I was with the Alpha there. I don't think that I never did just fire a man, but once that I remember, I fired three guys in one shot. I just told them what to do with them, I couldn't use them any longer. But, if I wanted to get rid of a man, I usually laid him off. I didn't just walk up and tell him you'd fire, take your time off us, only that one, one instance.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And these were, these were transient boys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they stealing from the company or something? Were they stealing or something?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, these kids, they had been, they came, they'd been over in the Yakima country and they were Mexican boys, there's four of them.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And they would come in and I was, I guess that was, and they was looking for jobs and they went down to the main office down there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And, well I said, well, he said, I don't know, I'll check my formula if I can get hold of him and see if he can, if he needs any help, any of the plants around there. And so he got hold of me at Troy. He told about these kids, he says, they're inexperienced. And he says, if you give them, if you want to kind of split them up in your, split them up with the other boys,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Then maybe you can get, maybe you can get the fire with them, maybe they'll be alright. And I told him to send them out to Joel and I'll go on up there and I can say I can use some help with that Joel and pilot, please. So I went up there and these kids come out here and they was, they had on old Oxfords and they didn't have no shoes. And so I got to split them up with the guys, I think I had about five men out there and I got to put them in a different car, we had a section.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And they was used to handling nothing like 125 pound sacks of peas like we was handling them, that's what I wanted to know from the peas. They wasn't used to that and they didn't like that and their hands got, they didn't like the hands. And then when, they thought that's too hard on their hands and then they had on these little Oxfords and good peas in their shoes and this and there and the other fellas, the other fellas, the other fellas, the other fellas, they got awful disgusted with them.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And so when, when I come they didn't have, they didn't have no money, they didn't have no place to stay.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So I brought them to town and I took them up there. I gave them a dinner, I think that was good for their dinner. So I told them well.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I said well, I'm telling you fellas, I says,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: you have to do better than you did this afternoon or I don't think I can use you anymore.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And there's two of them, well he says well, he's complaining about the shoes and they wanted me to buy them, wanted, wanted to know if I'd buy them some shoes.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well I told them I couldn't do that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I said if I, and I'm sure the company wouldn't stand for me to buy shoes for you.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And well, the two of them said well, we're going to see if we can get some lighter work and that's pretty heavy work for us. And I said okay, well I'll pick you up in the morning, well you fellas want to work so. So the next morning, well I don't know where they stayed at. And anyway the next morning I took them back out there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Given their assignments.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And about every,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You'd always come to me, you'd come around about this and this and that and the other, complaining about this and this and the other. The guys was giving them the heavy end of the work and all this and that and the other. So I just said well, I'll tell you.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I said you better go get in the pick up and I'll take you to town.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So I load them up and put them on into town. Got rid of them. That's about the only drive that I ever did was just out when I came. Then I had a father and son working for me. And he thought, he figured because his dad was working there, working for me. Because he figured well if I fired him, why his dad would quit too and that would throw me short handed.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So in the evening when he got time to go home I told him, I said Buster, I'm going to lay you off. If I need you anymore, I'll call you. He knew I needed you. I said if I need you, I'll give you a call.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So the next morning he didn't show up and neither did his dad.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So I went on that way before harvest before the end of the season is over. Then his dad came back and wanted to know if I put him back on for a while. So I did. He finished, came back and finished the season for me.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But as far as just losing my temper and going out in the can on those guys, something like that, I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have Gene any idea about your method of handling men? Or how you think as a supervisor that a good guy should do that?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I always figured that's one thing about my men. If the men had a gripe about working conditions or anything like that, I would take their gripe to the head man, to the company. I would go to bat for them. Actually, I would ask what they were talking about. I'd pick it up with the company. I'd go to bat for them. And I figured that's part of my duty. If the guys had a legitimate gripe about working conditions, sure. And I said that's one thing I did. Another thing about this is I never did, no man ever came to me and asked for a job that I just cut him right off the pockets. I don't say, well, I'm full handed today but I may be short tomorrow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So if you give me a phone number, why can't I reach you?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And we're taking a summer hot summertime. It's hot. When I come to town, sometimes I'll pick up a case of cool drinks or a watermelon or something like that. And I'll go out and take it off the roof and take the time out and have a drink of cool drinks or something like that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But I never did take no beer out, no drink, nothing like that out. I never did buy an old, especially what I have, a pile of watermelon cool drinks. And I bought watermelons and a seed and take them off and take away the melons. You can raise them.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: In other words, I always try to do what is right by the guys.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And that's the same way in an army. I was a sergeant in the army and then close the... I wasn't tough, but if I give an order, I stayed put until I saw that order was carried out. And then therefore they didn't take me for no joke when I gave them an order. I was pretty small. They didn't like that, some of them. They didn't think they had to. They found out that they had to take more than 10 miles of it. They gave us a title that good.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So, all in all, I never had too much trouble with men, people, anybody. I never had a couple of people.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I never had a couple of men. I didn't let those three sights go to my head.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Running the whole army.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was wondering, too, whether your parents gave you any instructions or taught you any ways of dealing with racial prejudice if you ran into it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: We never had any... I don't think we ever had any... we never had any... they never coursed us along those lines. I think it's when we ran into it, why we dealt with it in our own way. I don't remember my folks ever... but I know one thing. My folks was particular about those kids coming up, those little kids. If they had any trouble in the school or coming from school with kids...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: ... they would complain to them about this kid or this guy did sword and sword to me.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: The first thing they'd want to know, what did you do?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: What did you do to start this thing?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They always wanted to know both sides of the deal. Whether it was our folks... I know that's one thing they always... they wanted to find out about it. They didn't just...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But they'd... it was always this way. They always warned us kids...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: ... about fighting.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They didn't want us to start no fight.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And they didn't want us to run if it was pushed on to us.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They didn't want us to start no fight. Now that's one thing about the Settle boys... ... there's four of us and we were all pretty handy with the gloves. We had gloves hanging in the back porch and gloves hanging in the barn. We had an argument with each other and we said, okay, we'll get the gloves. That's why we settled out as two. We didn't get mad either. We sure used to knock the hell out of each other. We didn't. Just had a lot of arguments but we wasn't going mad. When we first of all gave us a pair of gloves for Christmas... ... and my dad told us then, he says, well...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: ... I don't want these gloves to get the boys into trouble.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He said, I don't care how much you use them. He said, if I ever hear of a kitchen area, use them on getting mad with them on... ... they go in the stove.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Getting mad with them on?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, yeah. No sir. If you want to just go out and have fun with them. But if you don't want to use them gloves getting mad with them on... ... he says they'll go in the stove. Or he said, if I ever hear of you starting a fight with them on the side, he says they'll go in the stove. My dad meant what he said too. We sure used to do some awful slugging each other. Knock each other around with those gloves on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you close enough to the same age and size that it wasn't too unfair?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I was about the smallest one. I was about the smallest one when I came to use my gloves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you fight in rounds or would you just put them on and sock it out?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, yeah. Just go to it. No, we didn't have no rounds. We didn't stop the round. The round was going on before we had enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You wouldn't have a brother as a ref either, would you?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You wouldn't have a brother stand between you to make... Oh no, no.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No. Oh, I used to... I used to... I used to referee these with... referee... kind of referee with them sometimes. But I wasn't in it. No, we had lots of fun with those gloves. We had them... as I say, we had them... ... there was a pair hanging on the back porch and there's always a pair out in the barn. And if you was a neighbor kid and a neighbor gets them up there and you thought you were pretty good at the glove... ... well, there's always one of the settle boys that gets a lot your size and takes you on.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, we had lots of fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Gene, when you said to me when we were talking before that it was about the worst setback you ever had...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: when you went in the Army and you got into a segregated unit like that... ...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say that, is that because that was the first time you really had to deal with that kind of prejudice?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I guess it was the first time that I ever ran into prejudice, but I think that hurt me worse than any of them. But I don't think that was the first time that I ever ran into them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind is it?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But I think that that is the first time that I ever went after something...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: ... or asked for something...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: ... and knowingly that I was turned down just because I was black.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you ever remember fighting with kids over prejudice when you were a kid?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, I never... tell the truth about it, I never did that. You take it in East Country School where I used to go to Country School down there. I never did have any fights. Now, my brother, next to my youngest brother, he was a hothead of the Civil War, I think. And he used to get in fights, but now I don't say that whether or not he wasn't always a color. He was just that kind of guy. You cross him up and do whatever the color of the wonder was. I don't remember fighting, really. I don't remember fighting. Only color was color, as long as I was brought up by prejudice, I don't remember.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Other different things come up and make you mad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Kids didn't think you ought to think you ought to, are you? Where were you in the order of the family? Where were you in the order of the family as age goes?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I'm the oldest one. Yeah, I'm the oldest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then was it your next brother that went to Virginia?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, it was the next one. My third brother, next to the youngest one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he the one that was a hothead of the Civil War?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, he was the hothead of the Civil War.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, he was one of them. He was one of the settled boys. He had more temper than the rest of us. He had to get down there and have to get a teacher down there in the school. He had a fighter too down there. Down there in the school where he went to down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to ask you how you met your wife.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did the two of you meet?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Her folks were farming down here at Dodge, Washington down below Lewiston down there. If you don't mind, Dodge is joking between Lewiston and Pumloy. They were farmers in there. When I first heard of them,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then in the later years, I went to the Army. I guess the year before I went to the Army.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They moved up here above Oakesdale. They had a farm up there. They were living there when I was in the Army. At that time, when I went to Camp Lewis, I guess there was a kid from a farming camp up here. She had worked for my wife's dad on the farm up there. Of course, he was telling me about his Army. In the meantime, while I was in the Army, my brother next to me and my sister,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: My brother got an old Model T Ford car while in service. Once they drove up there to meet the brothers, they met me. Then the year after, in 1919 I guess, next spring after Osweiler,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Her dad came to Moscow here for something. I don't remember what he came for here. He came down here to the Federal Land Bank. While he was here, he went out to the house to talk to my dad. My dad left my old camp.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: After the next week, I got the co -op to the service.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: In the winter, the first winter, I'll take it all the way. Then in the wintertime after I got out of service in 1919, I went to Spokane on Christmas holidays. When I went to Spokane, I met my wife's two brothers. They were in Spokane.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: The next spring was March or April. We had a new car. We got a new car that was in fall before that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Henry had a letter from the Federal Land Bank. He wanted my folks to come up and see him. So right after that we drove up there. I think it was March or April 1919. That's when I met him on the farm.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And it was about...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: We knew each other for quite a while. We never lost any blood. I guess about two years after that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: We saw each other every so often. Then our brother got sick.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I used to go up there to see him. Anyway, I had to take her out for a drive. I got there and took my wife out for a drive. There were two of the girls. The other one left and went to California. The oldest one went to California. My wife was there with the folks. We just kind of fooled around together until 1924. About three, two, three years. Anyway, from 1919 until 1924 before we got married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you seriously courting for much of that time?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, we wasn't seriously courting.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I was engaged to a minister's daughter in Spokane when I went into service. She wanted to get married before we went to service, but I wouldn't do that. Her dad wanted her to go to college. She thought she wanted to go to college one year anyway. So I didn't like this idea of just getting married and going into service. So, we didn't. When I got back, she had TV.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I saw it twice after I got home. Then her mother took her to Denver, that high climate down there. She was down there about a month. She died.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And, it's about a year, I just kind of played the field. And about in the next year or so, I started seeing more of my wife there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So, last winter we celebrated our 50th anniversary.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's really nice. Yes, last winter. We celebrated December 29th. We was married on December 24th.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: We celebrated our anniversary on the 29th, which was on Sunday.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Because we can celebrate our anniversary on the 29th. But we should have celebrated on the 26th. But the lady that was setting up the deal for us at the Methodist Church here, she thought we should have it on a Sunday. So, we gave it up on the 29th. We celebrated our 50th anniversary on the 29th of this past December.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you got married, did you have pretty strong feelings about having much to set yourself up for when you got married? Or did you feel you could do it on a, you know, it was okay to go ahead and do that on a shoestring?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, I figured I could. The rest of it, I figured I could too. So, I always got lots of confidence in myself.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But, did I say, I don't hardly think that I was raised on the farm all my life, but I really don't think I was put out to be a farmer. But I enjoy the farm life too, but it seems like it. I always wanted, I kind of wanted a job where that's...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I kind of wanted a kind of food -color job.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Something like that. Something like the meal clerk or something like that. I think I'd have...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But, as I always figured though, I always think the farm...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: ...is the most independent life of the land for me.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I know one thing about the farm. I think especially, especially for a black man, because he don't have to buck this labor market... ...he's more independent. I think that's one reason that I escaped so much of this prejudices. But all of us, the whole outfit is because we lived an independent life. We were dependent on somebody else all the time for our livelihood. We got it at the farm.
