That's one thing that I think the Seattle family escaped as much prejudice as they did here in the old white community. I think that's the way we got it all. We lost every farmer, but if I was... ...a good job, we would have felt a whole lot more. Just like you were a while ago. You got to have a record. You just got to be better, superior than the other fellas. And doing this job, you just don't get it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: We never...the labor market is something that we didn't buck. We didn't have that to buck. When I had the discrimination, it was social. We were being turned down at a restaurant or a hotel or something like that. That's why.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That didn't bother us either because we didn't stay in town. We didn't have them. We used to stay for no hotel. We'd come to town and we...the very cell of every went to a restaurant to eat. I don't think we ever did. I don't think I ever did. That's until after my wife and I was married. We used to go out and we used to come to town and see us. Come to town someday, we'd be in town. We'd probably go to the restaurant and we'd come to eat.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But I don't remember during that while, when my father used to work here. He used to bring us...some of us boys to town with him. We never went to no restaurant. Sometimes he'd buy some bologna or something like that. He'd eat on our way back home for us to just eat on our way back home. So, being a farmer, we missed a lot of this. We missed a lot of this for us, I think.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: As I say about my daughter,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She never run into it to see a day in action, race to the finish in action until after she got away from home and got to the city. That's what she saw.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: She went to find for schools down in California down there. This school is one school there up close to the University of California, not too far from the campus up there. They told her, her relatives, her aunts, told her there's no use going up there applying for a job because they had been different black teachers trying to get in up there and they just don't hire you up there. But, my daughter has a gate of her own and after she went to several other schools and didn't have any luck, she went up there. After she talked to the principal for about half an hour, the principal told her and says, as far as I'm concerned, you're hired. But I just got to go before the court and I let you know on a certain date. On that date somewhere, she called her and told her the job was hers. So, she stayed there for 10 years, she could have been there yet if she had wanted to, I guess.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's why I do everything, that's why the settles escape a lot of prejudice.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, another thing, I think it's not only that, we live on the farm and if I do see it,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: There's not too many families that conducted their self the way the settled family did, either black or white.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They had a, they lived a better than average standard of living, adopted that way of living, better than average. Especially for poor people, like we were. That's one thing about the settles,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: they had the respect of their fellow man.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They always had lots of respect and those that didn't care for the settled, it wasn't because of that, it was simply because they were black. That's the only thing we could save often, they are black. But outside of that, they can save a lot of that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: My dad was a poor man.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You know, he was a kid for kids. And he thought it, he thought us kids did the same thing, he didn't know it only.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So when my dad went to his grave, he left a good thing for the settled and I tried my best to hold it up since then. You don't hear of any families, any people, the new wife is to save.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I heard nothing but good about the settled family, what I heard. Well, that's what my dad, he's leading that, he's leading that, he treats that best for us and his family. He never liked the old monkey business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you say conduct, or conducting themselves, the family, do you mean the way you treat other people?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, we are together.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And being honest and reliable.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And being honest and reliable.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, now I heard my dad argue that, talk about that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He seems to think, if I remember correctly, he thinks that out west here, there are so many people. Well, you know, there's people here, kids that go to manhood, you might say, that never had any contact with a black person. It's just like my sister now, she grew up here and she went to work at a fraternity house over there in Pullman in her younger days. She was a pretty old girl, took her fraternity time over there. And she said some of those kids would come and talk to her about this colored racial business. And you know, she says, you know, we never grew up in a little town down here in Washington, you know, they never. And she said, you know, we never had no contact with no colored person, black person. There wasn't none around. So,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: It was kind of a,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: The thing was with them, they had to learn you. Just like I said when I started high school here. Some of them would stand in the wall and kind of stare at you and look, but as soon as you found out that you were, the only difference was, why I was black and they was white, was that I was still a human being. Why then, that kind of warmed up to you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your father's opinion of that then?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were saying your father talked about that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, well he said, well he figured, he figured out, you take these people that grew up out in the west that had never no contact with the black person. He kind of figured that they'd have more of a tendency towards prejudice than the southerners that grew up with them and understood that they grew up.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But here, they got to find you out. They don't know nothing about you. You got to prove yourself.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's the idea, you got to prove yourself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the difference between the Settle family and the Wells family up that dairy well?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: There's no bigger hearted family in the country better than the Wells family. Well, they were just a little bit different than the Settle family. They were more of a rowdy and drinking once and...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Hi, how are you?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: This is my wife, Laura.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How are you? How's your arm?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Oh, getting better. Oh, that's well. It's pretty good, man.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Well, I thought I was in the wrong house. Yeah, we sort of sit out here and shade the tree. It's nice and clean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't this a beautiful tree? Isn't this a nice tree?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Look at it, it shades the whole yard.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Gene complains about it, but I think he likes it anyway. Yeah, I think he likes it. It's kind of dirty. The leaves start falling. See these leaves falling on my own. Then they have these little green... I should have it sprayed for little bugs to get on the ball. They're not too bad right now, but they get pretty bad. I just put in a lot of time out here. I do quite a lot of reading and I... Sam told me about the tree when he came out here the first time. He was pretty impressed by it. Yeah, I like it now. I'm already... The only thing I don't have is a TV. I like to have my TV out there. Because you see there's going to be a football game starting pretty soon. So when I get the baseball game out, I'm getting a little TV. We eat out here quite a bit. We used to. We wouldn't go to... Nats get too bad. We'd spend a lot of time with neighbors coming over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Gene, I didn't get nearly through all the things I was going to talk to you about. Well, I'll come back again. I don't want to do what happens at home. After Laura's folks are gone, after my vacation, I will.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, anytime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a hard time doing the laundry?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, it took me a long time. I didn't get it. She heard her doing it with just one hand. I can use it some, but I can't carry weight in it yet. Because my wrist is still weak. Yeah, you work your fingers not to well. I think people thought I was crazy in there. It took me about 20 minutes to get the wash going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They're probably thinking, what kind of husband has she got?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't do that.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Maybe I shouldn't go back and help you. Help you fold it, because they'll know they'll see me then. They wonder why I do that all the time. I get all these dirty lips.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: It'd be a different crew. Boy, that place is creepy.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: It's nice here than in the laundromat. It's cool here. Last time I was here, it was so hot. It was just a hot week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Remember?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was really cool as long as we're underneath this tree. I started to walk home, back to the office. I almost fell in love. It was so hot. Well, I think you started getting kind of stuck in here. I'd just get the hose and water the lawn around here.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I'd go down in front of you and wash it off. It was cool. I stay out here most of the time. My wife gets out here sometime or two. She likes to miss her soap operas and TVs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the college radio station that you were listening to when I came over?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, that was on there. That was WSU.
